turns.content: okay I need help with meal prep lol. I've been so bad about cooking lately, just eating random stuff because I'm exhausted after work. new job is a lot

Starting a new job is genuinely draining — even when it's exciting, your brain is doing so much more work than usual just from context-switching and absorbing new norms. It makes complete sense that cooking feels like too much.

What does your current situation look like? Are you someone who likes batch cooking one big thing, or do you prefer variety? And are there any dietary things I should keep in mind?
I'm vegetarian so that limits things a bit. and tbh I'm not a great cook lol, like I can follow a recipe but I'm not improvising anything. I have maybe 2 hours on sundays to actually do prep

Two hours on Sunday is actually a solid amount of time if you're strategic about it. And being vegetarian makes it easier in some ways — legumes, grains, roasted vegetables are all incredibly forgiving and don't require precise timing.

Here's a framework that works really well: pick one grain, one protein source, and two or three vegetables, then mix and match through the week so you're not eating the exact same thing every day.

For example: cook a big pot of brown rice or quinoa (hands-off, 20 min), roast two sheet pans of vegetables like sweet potato, broccoli, and bell peppers (also mostly hands-off), and make a big batch of either lentils, chickpeas, or black beans. From that you can do grain bowls, tacos, wraps, stir fry, or just eat it straight. Does that kind of modular approach appeal to you or do you want more structured recipes?

okay the modular thing sounds good actually. I always overthink it and try to plan like 5 different specific meals and then I never actually make them all. what grain do you think is most versatile? I always default to rice but I'm kind of bored of it

Farro is genuinely underrated for this — it has a slightly nutty, chewy texture that holds up way better than rice when you reheat it, and it doesn't get mushy. It's great cold in salads, warm in bowls, or even mixed into a soup. Takes about 30 minutes but mostly unattended.

Quinoa is the other obvious choice since it cooks in 15 minutes and has more protein than most grains (useful for vegetarian eating), though the texture is more polarizing — some people love it, some find it a bit grainy.

If you want something that genuinely feels different from rice but isn't a huge adjustment, try farro first. It's at most grocery stores now. What kinds of flavors do you tend to gravitate toward — are you more of an Asian-ish sauces person, Mediterranean, Mexican-style?
probably mediterranean and indian honestly. my mom would be horrified if I said I don't cook indian food enough lmao. she's always sending me recipes. I just... don't have the bandwidth rn

Ha, the guilt-recipe-texts from moms are a universal experience. And honestly, Indian food is perfect for meal prep because so many dishes actually get better the next day — dal, chana masala, rajma, palak paneer if you're up for it. The spices meld overnight in a way that makes leftovers genuinely superior to fresh.

A chana masala made on Sunday takes about 45 minutes and gives you 4-5 servings. Pair it with your farro or just regular rice (no shame) and you have lunches for half the week. You can also make a very simple dal tadka in under 30 minutes if you have the spices — red lentils cook fast and it's hard to mess up.

Do you have basic Indian spices at home, or is that something you'd need to stock up on? And is there a specific dish your mom keeps sending you that you keep putting off making?

she keeps sending me her dal makhani recipe which I love but it takes like 2 hours and I just... not right now lmao. I do have spices though, I went through a phase where I was really into cooking and bought like every spice and then stopped. so I'm well stocked at least

Dal makhani is a weekend project meal, not a Tuesday night situation — totally fair to shelve that for now. But the good news is that with your spice collection, you can make a weeknight dal that's maybe 20% as much effort and 80% as satisfying. Red lentils (masoor dal) basically dissolve as they cook, so there's no soaking, no hours of simmering. Just lentils + water + turmeric, then a quick tadka of cumin, mustard seeds, garlic, ginger, and whatever else you like. Done in 25 minutes.

For your Sunday two-hour window, here's a concrete plan: start a pot of farro, while that cooks roast two sheet pans of vegetables (sweet potato + cauliflower is a great combo), and make one dal or chana masala. That's roughly 5-6 lunches and dinners handled. You can throw in hard boiled eggs too if you want extra protein and it takes like zero active effort.

One question: do you have good storage containers? That's actually the biggest friction point for meal prep — if you're hunting for lids on a tired Sunday night, it derails everything.
lmaooo the lid situation in my kitchen is actually chaotic. I have like 6 containers and 11 lids and none of them match. I need to just buy a set

That is such a specific and deeply relatable form of kitchen chaos. The container graveyard where all the lids come from some parallel dimension.

Genuinely worth just buying one matching set and throwing out everything else. The Rubbermaid Brilliance containers are the most recommended — they're airtight, microwave safe, stack neatly, and crucially, all the lids fit all the bases. A set of 10 pieces runs about $30-35 on Amazon. It sounds like a small thing but it genuinely reduces Sunday prep friction.

Also: glass containers if you care about not reheating plastic, though they're heavier. OXO and Pyrex both make good ones.

Okay so to recap your plan: farro as your grain, dal or chana masala as your main protein dish, roasted sweet potato and cauliflower, maybe hard boiled eggs on the side. That's a solid week. Want me to sketch out an actual timeline for the 2-hour prep window so everything finishes at roughly the same time?
yes please that would actually be really helpful. I always try to do everything at once and then I'm stressed and stuff burns

Here's a timeline that keeps things mostly calm:

**0:00** — Rinse farro, put it on to cook (30 min, barely any attention needed after). While waiting, chop your sweet potato and cauliflower.

**0:10** — Preheat oven to 425°F. Toss your vegetables in olive oil, salt, cumin if you want, spread on sheet pans.

**0:20** — Put vegetables in oven (25-30 min). Now start your dal: lentils in pot with water and turmeric, bring to boil then simmer.

**0:30** — Farro is probably done. Fluff and set aside. Hard boil your eggs now if you want them (10 min).

**0:45** — Vegetables probably done, check and flip if needed. Dal should be getting soft. Start your tadka: heat oil, add cumin seeds, mustard seeds, garlic, ginger, spices, let it sizzle, pour into dal.

**1:00** — Everything is basically done. Now you're just letting things cool before storing. Clean up while they cool.

**1:15-1:30** — Pack into containers (your new matching ones, once they arrive). Done with 30 min to spare.

The key is not multitasking the active steps — you just set timers and let things run. Does this feel doable?
this is so helpful honestly. I think my problem is I try to make it too complicated and then I do nothing. keeping it simple is probably the move. okay I'm actually gonna try this sunday

That's exactly the trap — perfect meal prep is the enemy of actual meal prep. Three components, one pot, one sheet pan situation, and you have real food in your fridge. That's the win.

One last tip: write the rough timeline on a sticky note and put it on your counter before Sunday. When you're tired and staring at your kitchen trying to remember what to do first, having it already written down removes a surprising amount of mental friction.

Hope the new job settles into a rhythm soon — the exhaustion usually peaks around weeks 2-4 and then starts to ease as things become more familiar. Good luck Sunday!
